timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

heb.paragraph: The Recabites are descendants of Jonadab son of Rekab (Recab), who commanded his family to abstain from wine, never build houses, never sow seed, never plant vineyards, but live in tents (vv.6-7). For over 200 years they have obeyed their ancestor's command. God brings Jeremiah to test them: he offers them wine in the temple. They refuse.

panels.ctx: The Recabite episode is a brilliant object lesson in contrasting faithfulness. A nomadic clan obeys a human ancestor's commands for two centuries, while Israel disobeys the living God's commands from the moment they are given. The Recabites' faithfulness is not held up as a religious ideal -- their abstinence is cultural, not theological -- but as a shaming comparison. If humans can obey a human father so consistently, why can't Israel obey God?

cross.note: Jehonadab son of Rekab -- the ancestor who established the Recabite way of life and supported Jehu's purge of Baal worship.
Jesus contrasts human traditions with God's commandments -- the same dynamic Jeremiah exploits with the Recabites.

notes.note: God commands Jeremiah to bring the Recabites into a temple chamber and offer them wine. The test is designed to fail -- God knows they will refuse -- because their refusal will indict Israel.
Jonadab's commands: no wine, no houses, no agriculture, tent-dwelling only. These are not divine commands but family tradition. Yet they are obeyed absolutely for over 200 years.
The Recabites have come into Jerusalem only because of the Babylonian invasion -- their tent-dwelling lifestyle has been disrupted by war. Even under pressure, they maintain the wine prohibition.

Calvin admires the Recabites' consistency but clarifies: "Their obedience is praiseworthy as filial loyalty, not as religious obligation. The point is the contrast: if human authority commands such fidelity, how much more should divine authority."
The "room" (lishka) where the Recabites are brought was a chamber in the temple complex used by priests and officials. Offering wine in a temple setting adds a quasi-sacral dimension to the test.
Lundbom identifies this as a "test oracle" where the outcome is predetermined: the Recabites will pass, Israel will fail. The test is for the audience's benefit, not for the Recabites'.
Brueggemann reads the Recabite episode as an indictment of "selective obedience." Israel obeys nothing; the Recabites obey everything. The contrast demolishes every excuse.

heb.paragraph: "Will you not learn a lesson (musar) and obey my words?" (v.13). The word musar carries the dual sense of discipline and instruction. Israel has received both -- corrective punishment and prophetic teaching -- and has responded to neither. The Recabites needed only their ancestor's word; Israel has had God's word and still refuses.

panels.ctx: The application is devastating: "Jonadab's descendants have carried out the command their forefather gave them, but these people have not obeyed me" (v.16). The Recabites receive a permanent blessing: "Jonadab will never fail to have a descendant to serve me" (v.19). Their faithfulness to a human command earns divine guarantee. Israel's unfaithfulness to the divine command earns destruction.

cross.note: "We have human fathers who disciplined us and we respected them. How much more should we submit to the Father of spirits?" -- the same argument from lesser to greater.
"Again and again I spoke to you, but you did not listen" -- the refrain of rejected prophetic speech that the Recabites' obedience throws into relief.

notes.note: The qal wahomer (lesser-to-greater) argument: Jonadab spoke once and was obeyed for centuries. God has spoken "again and again" through prophets and been ignored every time.
Therefore: the disaster comes not because God failed to speak but because Israel failed to listen. Communication was not the problem; reception was.
"Jonadab will never fail to have a descendant to serve me." The Recabites receive the permanence promise that the Davidic dynasty has forfeited through disobedience.

Calvin develops the argument: "If obedience to a dead ancestor produces such consistency, what would obedience to the living God produce? The Recabites shame every generation that claims God's commands are too difficult."
"To serve me" (la'amod lephonay) literally means "to stand before me" -- language normally used for priestly or prophetic service. The Recabites receive a quasi-priestly blessing.

Lundbom notes that the Recabite blessing (v.19) mirrors the Davidic covenant language -- "never fail to have a descendant." Faithful obedience to human authority receives the same guarantee that unfaithful kings forfeited.
Brueggemann contrasts the two outcomes: "Israel receives the judgment it was warned about. The Recabites receive a permanence they never requested. Obedience is its own argument."

rows.label: vv.1-5
rows.text: The Test: Wine Offered to the Recabites

lit.note: The Recabite episode is a "test oracle" -- the predetermined result shames Israel through contrast. Human-to-human obedience exceeds divine-to-human obedience.

themes.note: Faith peaks at 9 -- the Recabites' 200-year obedience is the foil for Israel's chronic unfaithfulness.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Recabites' Faithfulness

translations.text: We have obeyed everything our forefather Jehonadab son of Rekab commanded us. Neither we nor our wives nor our sons and daughters have ever drunk wine
We have obeyed the voice of Jonadab the son of Rechab, our father, in all that he commanded us, to drink no wine all our days, ourselves, our wives, our sons, or our daughters,
“Go to the Rekabite family and invite them to come to one of the side rooms of the house of the Lord and give them wine to drink.”
“Go to the house of the Rechabites and speak with them and bring them to the house of the LORD, into one of the chambers; then offer them wine to drink.”
